title: Melitzanosalata
subtitle: Greek smoky aubergine dip
excerpt: Aubergines charred directly over a flame until the skin blackens and the flesh collapses, then beaten with garlic, lemon, and olive oil into a smoky, silky dip.
sourceNotes: Melitzanosalata (from melitzana, aubergine, and salata, salad) appears across the eastern Mediterranean and Balkans. Documented in Diane Kochilas, The Glorious Foods of Greece (2001) and Claudia Roden, A New Book of Middle Eastern Food (1985). The key technique is open-flame charring to develop the smoky flavour that distinguishes it from simply baked aubergine.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps covered in the fridge for up to 2 days. The smoke flavour intensifies overnight.

content.text: The smoke is the whole point. Melitzanosalata made from oven-roasted aubergine is pleasant; made from aubergines charred directly over a gas flame, it is something else. The skin scorches, the flesh softens to near-liquid, and a deep, woody smoke penetrates the entire vegetable.

On a gas hob, this takes 15 to 20 minutes per aubergine, turning every few minutes with tongs. Under a grill on high, it works but lacks the intensity. Outdoors over a charcoal or wood fire, it is exceptional. The flesh must drain in a colander for at least 15 minutes after charring — the liquid that runs off is intensely bitter and ruins the dip if left in.

Set 3 aubergines directly on the gas flame over medium heat. Turn every 4 to 5 minutes with tongs until the skin is completely blackened on all sides and the aubergines have collapsed and feel very soft when pressed, 15 to 20 minutes total. Alternatively, char under a very hot grill, turning every 5 minutes.

Transfer to a colander set over a bowl. When cool enough to handle, peel off the charred skin — it should slip away easily. Pull open the flesh and let it drain in the colander for at least 15 minutes. Discard all the dark, bitter liquid that drains off.

Chop the drained flesh roughly — or beat with a fork for a slightly smoother result. Add the garlic, olive oil, lemon juice, vinegar if using, parsley, and salt. Mix well. Add the red onion if using. Taste and adjust seasoning. Serve with pitta or bread, a drizzle of olive oil, and a pinch of paprika.

items.cause: Not drained long enough; the bitter liquid wasn't removed
items.fix: Drain for at least 15 minutes and discard all liquid; squeeze the flesh gently to press out more

items.cause: Oven-roasted rather than flame-charred, or not all the skin was fully blackened
items.fix: Ensure the skin is fully charred on all sides; partial charring gives partial smoke
Continue charring until the aubergine feels completely soft and collapsed when pressed — a partially firm aubergine is undercooked

items.cause: Raw garlic is sharp; 2 cloves is the standard amount
items.fix: Reduce to 1 clove; or roast the garlic alongside the aubergine for a milder, sweeter flavour

content.text: Melitzanosalata is the summer companion to tzatziki and taramasalata — always on the same mezze table, always eaten with the same pitta. The dish is closely related to the Middle Eastern baba ganoush and the Romanian zacuscă, and all three use the same core technique of open-fire charring. Greek versions tend to be simpler than the Levantine ones, avoiding tahini or cumin, letting the smoke and olive oil carry the flavour.
